claims.claim_text: Available GPU memory strongly constrains whether a large model fits locally and whether quantization is needed to reduce memory footprint.
claims.span_hint: lines 48-59 | section: VRAM Is the First Hard Limit
claims.rationale: The Transformers bitsandbytes documentation explicitly says quantization reduces memory requirements and makes large models easier to fit on limited hardware.

claims.claim_text: Batch size is constrained by GPU memory during local training workloads.
claims.span_hint: lines 52-57 | section: VRAM Is the First Hard Limit
claims.rationale: The official Transformers GPU training guide states that batch size affects memory usage and that the feasible batch size depends on the GPU.

claims.claim_text: Longer sequence lengths can drive memory use high enough to cause out-of-memory errors.
claims.span_hint: line 56 | section: VRAM Is the First Hard Limit
claims.rationale: The TRL memory guide explains that large max_length values can spike memory usage and lead to OOM errors.

claims.claim_text: When GPU memory is insufficient, frameworks can offload model weights between GPU and CPU/system memory.
claims.span_hint: line 71 | section: System RAM Is the Second Wall
claims.rationale: The bitsandbytes documentation explicitly documents CPU offloading to fit large models into memory when GPU capacity is limited.

claims.claim_text: Quantized LoRA-style fine-tuning can make single-GPU fine-tuning practical for some model sizes.
claims.span_hint: lines 138-139, 165 | sections: What Different Workloads Actually Need; Sizing Rules That Age Well
claims.rationale: The QLoRA paper shows that quantized LoRA-style fine-tuning can reduce memory use enough to make single-GPU fine-tuning feasible.

claims.claim_text: 16 GB is workable for light learning but restrictive.
claims.anchor_text: - `16 GB` is workable for light learning, but restrictive
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Lower-memory systems can work for light learning, but they become restrictive quickly
claims.rationale: This is a precise threshold presented as a general rule without an authoritative source.

claims.claim_text: 32 GB is a realistic floor for comfortable local AI work.
claims.anchor_text: - `32 GB` is a realistic floor for comfortable local AI work
claims.suggested_rewrite: - A moderate amount of RAM gives much more headroom for comfortable local AI work
claims.rationale: The exact floor is a workload-dependent heuristic, not a source-backed universal threshold.

claims.claim_text: 64 GB becomes attractive once you run local databases, heavier notebooks, and multiple services together.
claims.anchor_text: - `64 GB` becomes attractive once you run local databases, heavier notebooks, and multiple services together
claims.suggested_rewrite: - Higher RAM capacity becomes attractive once you run local databases, heavier notebooks, and multiple services together
claims.rationale: This is another uncited exact capacity threshold that varies by workload and toolchain.

further_reading.title: Transformers Bitsandbytes Quantization Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Best current reference for how quantization and CPU offloading change local model-fit constraints on limited hardware.

further_reading.title: Transformers GPU Training Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Explains the single-GPU memory tradeoffs behind batch size, gradient accumulation, and efficient local training.

further_reading.title: QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs
further_reading.why_relevant: Primary paper for why quantized LoRA-style fine-tuning materially changes what a single GPU can handle.

notes: Skipped quiz items, quiz answers, hands-on exercise steps, and command blocks. No section citation pool exists for ai-ml-engineering/prerequisites, so all cited claims rely on newly proposed URLs.
